text:
Limited  proteolysis-coupled  mass  spectrometry  captures proteome-wide protein structural alterations and biomolecular condensation in living cells

Franziska Elsässer 1 , Roberta Florea 1 , Felix Räsch 2 , Karsten Weis 1 , Natalie de Souza 1 , and Paola Picotti 1

1 Institute of Molecular Systems Biology, Department of Biology, ETH Zürich

2 Institute of Biochemistry, Department of Biology, ETH Zürich

Abstract

The function of a protein is determined by its structure, which may change dynamically in  response  to  post-translational  modifications,  interaction  with  other  molecules,  or environmental factors like temperature. Limited proteolysis-coupled mass spectrometry (LiP-MS) captures such structural alterations on a proteome-wide scale via the detection of altered protease susceptibility patterns of proteins. However, this technique  has  so  far  required  cell  lysis,  which  exposes  proteins  to  non-native conditions and  can  disrupt labile interactions such  as  those  occurring  within biomolecular  condensates.  To  study  protein  structures  directly  within  cells,  we developed in-cell LiP-MS. We optimized conditions for introduction of proteinase K into human cells using electroporation and validated that intracellular cleavage occurs. Incell LiP-MS captured not only the known binding of rapamycin to FKBP1A within the cell, but also downstream effects of pathway activation. Moreover, it detected global protein  structural  alterations  upon  sodium  arsenite  treatment  and  captured  the structural  dynamics  of  hundreds  of  proteins  from  biomolecular  condensates  with peptide level resolution and within live human cells. We detected known and novel structural alterations of proteins from stress granules as well as from nuclear speckles and validated alteration of nuclear speckles by fluorescence microscopy. Our dataset provides a resource describing the structural changes of human proteins in response to cellular stress and pinpoints structurally altered regions. Further, comparison of LiPbased structural fingerprints before and after cell lysis revealed which human proteins are susceptible to structural change upon cell lysis, therefore guiding the design of future experiments requiring native protein structures.

Introduction

Proteins serve many functions in cells, regulating growth, differentiation, and survival. Since protein structure is a key determinant of protein function, the study of protein structure before and after various perturbations (e.g., stress, disease, or treatment with drugs) yields insight into the understanding of protein function and dysfunction in health and  disease.  Structural  predictions  based  on  Alphafold  or  RoseTTAFold  are  now available for over 98% of human proteins (Baek et al., 2021; Tunyasuvunakool et al., 2021),  complementing  the  large  body  of  experimentally  solved  protein  structures. However, most available protein structures are a static snapshot, and information on protein structural changes between conditions is very limited.

Techniques to determine high-resolution protein structures typically require that the proteins or protein complexes of interest are purified. Such in vitro analyses do not recapitulate  important  properties  of  the  cellular  environment  such  as  molecular crowding, presence of small-molecule or macromolecule interactors, and subcellular gradients of pH or metabolites. Also, certain features of protein structure depend on subcellular localization and might be altered upon cell lysis. Low-affinity or dynamic interactions, such as those within biomolecular condensates, can be lost or difficult to capture upon cell lysis also due to dilution of the cellular content (Jain et al., 2016). It is therefore important to study protein dynamics within the native cellular environment.

In-cell  nuclear  magnetic  resonance  (NMR)  and  Förster  resonance  energy  transfer (FRET)  techniques  provide  information  on  the  dynamics  of  protein  structures  with atomic resolution inside cells (Ha et al., 1999; Sakakibara et al., 2009), but typically one protein is studied at a time, both techniques require labeling, and FRET requires extensive  optimization.  Cryo-electron  tomography  can  be  used  to  solve  protein structures in their cellular context, but studying protein dynamics remains challenging, and the approach is not applicable on a proteome-wide scale.

We previously developed limited proteolysis coupled mass spectrometry (LiP-MS), a structural  proteomics  approach  that  allows  the  study  of  structural  changes  in thousands of proteins simultaneously with peptide-level resolution (Feng et al., 2014). In LiP-MS, native proteins are cleaved by a non-specific protease for a short period of time,  followed  by  trypsin  digestion  under  denaturing  conditions,  which  results  in structure-specific  peptide  patterns  that  can  be  quantitatively  read  out  by  mass spectrometry (Figure 1a). We have shown that LiP-MS can detect numerous molecular events,  including  allosteric  regulation,  changes  in  enzyme  activity,  protein-protein interactions, post-translational modification, protein-small molecule interactions, and protein aggregation events(Cappelletti et al., 2021; Piazza et al., 2018). LiP-MS has been applied for drug target identification  (Piazza et al.,  2020)  and  to  characterize structural  changes  in  biofluids  of  individuals  suffering  from  Parkinson's  disease (Mackmull et al., 2022). Although powerful, LiP-MS has so far only been applicable to cell or tissue lysates (Malinovska et al., 2023; Schopper et al., 2017), where various protein structures may be altered due to the loss of intracellular conditions.

Here we describe our development of in-cell LiP-MS. The in-cell analysis is enabled by delivery of proteinase K (PK), a low specificity protease, into living mammalian cells using  an  electroporation  approach  optimized  to  ensure  rapid  (millisecond)  and reproducible intracellular uptake of the protease and the desired level of intracellular cleavage. We  detected PK-induced LiP signals across the proteome in an electroporation-dependent manner and validated the delivery of active protease into cells using an intracellular sensor based on FRET. In-cell LiP-MS captured the wellcharacterized  and  high-affinity  interaction  of  rapamycin  with  the  protein  FKBP1A (Heitman et al., 1991) as was previously observed with standard LiP-MS in cell lysates (Piazza  et al., 2020). In-cell  LiP  also  revealed  dynamics  within  biomolecular condensates that are formed in part through low-affinity interactions. Under arsenite stress,  in-cell  LiP-MS  captured  structural  alterations  in  known  and  novel  stress granule-related proteins as well as in nuclear speckle proteins, which we could validate with fluorescence microscopy. Our study provides the first available resource of in vivo proteome-wide protein structural changes during cellular stress, identifies proteins that undergo structural alterations upon cell lysis, and pinpoints structurally altered regions in proteins, including those involved in biomolecular condensation. Further, the in-cell LiP-MS method will allow the study of protein structural changes under any conditions of interest within living cells with peptide-level resolution and on a proteome-wide scale.

Results

Development of in-cell LiP-MS

To enable the study of protein structural states in their native cellular environment, we aimed to identify electroporation conditions that reliably introduce sufficient PK into mammalian cells to yield structurally informative cleavages with good coverage of the proteome. We  detached HEK293  cells from the culture dish, washed, and resuspended in PBS (Figure 1b). Immediately after addition of PK, cells were subjected to electroporation and then incubated for 1 min at 37 °C to allow limited proteolysis of intracellular  proteins.  Extracellular  PK  was  removed  by  washing,  PK  activity  was quenched by addition of a chaotrope and heating to 98 °C, and samples were snapfrozen. Cells were subsequently lysed, trypsin treated, and quantitative, label-free MS analysis was performed.

To assess whether PK had been delivered into cells, we quantified the fully tryptic peptides (FT; i.e., those with two tryptic ends) and the half-tryptic peptides (HT, i.e., those with only a single tryptic end) under various conditions, thus using the proteolytic cleavage  itself  as  a  readout  of  PK  delivery.  In  contrast  to  trypsin,  which  cleaves preferably after lysine and arginine residues, PK has low sequence specificity so that a peptide with one or two non-tryptic termini is likely to be generated by PK cleavage. Therefore, an increase in HT peptide intensity and a decrease in FT peptide intensity in a sample sequentially digested with PK and trypsin, relative to a sample digested with trypsin alone, reports on PK cleavage events. We indeed observed evidence of PK cleavage in cells electroporated in the presence of PK compared to cells incubated with  PK  but  not  electroporated  (Figure  1c).  PK  cleavages  were  detected  on  2491 proteins.  Without  electroporation,  addition  of  PK  to  cells  did  not  lead  to  significant increased proteolytic cleavage over control (no PK; Figure 1d). That PK indeed enters the  cells  upon  electroporation  was  further  supported  by  gene  ontology  enrichment analysis, which showed that proteins with peptide signatures indicative of PK cleavage were  significantly  enriched  in  cytosolic  and  nucleus-associated  terms  (Figure  1f). Further,  there  appears  to  be  no  substantial  leakage  of  intracellular  proteins  upon electroporation since the protein content of the cell supernatant was not changed by electroporation (Figure S1a).

In order to identify conditions that result in rapid and reproducible intracellular delivery of PK with minimal electroporation-induced effects on cellular physiology, we examined proteome-wide changes in peptide and protein levels upon electroporation alone. We observed  no  significant  electroporation-induced  changes  with  one  25-ms  pulse  at 1000 V or with up to two 25-ms pulses at 800 V (Figure 1e, Figure S1b). Moreover, FT and HT peptides were symmetrically distributed in all tested electroporation settings, suggesting that electroporation alone does not cause protein cleavage (Figure S1b, e).

Under higher voltage and with more pulses, we observed a drop in peptide and protein intensity relative to the non-electroporated control (Figure S1b, c), suggesting that high voltage induces protein aggregation or cleavage. Cell viability as assayed with trypan blue staining did not significantly decrease within 5 min after electroporation with one 25-ms pulse of 1000 V (ANOVA q-value < 0.05; Figure S1d), and we chose these conditions for subsequent experiments.

In order to maximize proteome coverage of structurally-informative PK cleavages, we then  optimized  the  enzyme-to-substrate  ratio  and  determined  that  extracellular  PK concentrations of 100 μM for 3 x 10 6  cells led to the highest number of reproducible intracellular cleavages (Figure 1g). Although more cleaved peptides were detected at higher PK concentrations with up to 17% of peptides being HT at 1000 μM PK (30% half-tryptic  peptides  is  typical  in  a  standard  LiP-MS  experiment  in  lysate),  the reproducibility of cleavage decreased compared to 100 μM PK (Figure S2a-c), possibly because of over-digestion. Hence, we chose 100 µM extracellular PK for electroporation  in  subsequent  experiments.  We  further  increased  the  number  of reproducible electroporation-dependent PK cleavages by optimizing incubation time and  number  of  replicates  (Figure  S3a).  Using  a  2-minute  incubation  time  and  six replicates per condition, 17050 peptides on 3616 of 4760 detected proteins changed significantly  relative  to  control  (i.e.,  PK  added,  no  electroporation;  Figure  S3a), corresponding to 7182 distinct PK cleavage sites on 2367 proteins (Figure 1h; S3a). The coverage in an experiment comparing two perturbed cell states may be higher, since coverage is affected statistically by the fact that several species of HT peptides are not detected in the non-electroporation control; this is expected to be less of an issue  when  two  electroporated  samples  are  being  compared.  Prolonging  limited proteolysis  to  5  minutes  did  not  further  increase  PK  cleavage  (Figure  S3b),  and reducing  it  yielded  lower  digestion  reproducibility.  To  conclude,  we  have  identified conditions  to  deliver  PK  into  HEK293  cells  to  generate  reproducible  PK-induced cleavages in about 1600 mainly intracellular proteins without unduly compromising cell viability or the cellular proteome.

Validation of in-cell LiP-MS

To  orthogonally  test that PK  enters  cells upon  electroporation  and  is  active intracellularly, we used HEK293 cells expressing a FRET-based sensor of protease activity. The sensor consists of ECFP and YPet proteins separated by a short linker sequence  (Gray  et  al.,  2010).  Upon  excitation  of  ECFP,  yellow  YPet  fluorescence indicates  an  intact  sensor,  whereas  cleavage  of  the  linker  region  between  the  two fluorescent proteins results in loss of FRET signal and therefore an increase in the ECFP fluorescence relative to that of YPet. We first confirmed that the sensor behaves as expected upon addition of PK to a native lysate of the reporter cells. We observed cleavage within minutes of adding PK, and exposing the protease to our electroporation conditions (one 25-ms pulse of 1000 V) did not affect its activity (Figure 2a).  Electroporation  of  PK  into  cells  also  resulted  in  an  increase  in  the  ECFP fluorescence relative to that of YPet, confirming that active PK enters the cells and cleaves the reporter protein (Figure 2b). The larger variation we observe in the in-cell context are likely to be due to cell-to-cell variability, for example in sensor expression and PK internalization, as well as technical variability introduced by the electroporation procedure. Neither addition of  PK  without  electroporation  nor  electroporation  alone affected the sensor fluorescence signal (Figure 2b). These data provide orthogonal evidence that electroporation allows the introduction of active PK into mammalian cells.

We estimated the number of PK molecules that enter the cell upon electroporation under  our  chosen  experimental  conditions  by  comparison  to  a  calibration  curve prepared by analysis of lysates containing four accurately quantified heavy-labeled PKspecific peptides. Cells were treated with PK and subjected to electroporation; control cells were treated with the protease but not electroporated, after which both treated and control cells were thoroughly washed to remove extracellular PK. By comparing to the  calibration  curve,  we  calculated  that  the  electroporated  sample  contained 52.5 pmol PK and that the control sample contained 16.0 pmol PK (Figure S4a). Since the control cells were not electroporated, this is likely to be extracellular PK that was not  removed  during  washing,  and  should  not  be  able  to  cleave  the  intracellular proteome. We reasoned that the difference (36.6 pmol) corresponds to intracellular PK; note that we had initially added 12 nmol of PK to the cells.

Next,  we  asked  whether  in-cell  LiP-MS  can  capture  known  structural  alterations associated  to  high-affinity  binding  events  by  making  use  of  the  well-characterized binding of rapamycin to the protein FKBP1A. After treatment with 100 nM rapamycin for  5 minutes,  cells  were  electroporated  with  PK  and  processed  following  the previously optimized workflow. We detected an FKPB1A-derived peptide as one of the strongest hits in a comparison of peptide intensities between rapamycin-treated and control (DMSO-treated) cells (Figure 2c; FC > 1.5, p-value < 0.01, n = 6 replicates). Further, this peptide mapped close to the known rapamycin binding site on FKPB1A (Figure 2d). Similar results were obtained for cells treated with 10 μM rapamycin for 10 minutes prior to electroporation of PK (Figure S4c, c; FC > 1.5, q-value < 0.05, n = 6 replicates).  Standard  LiP-MS  of  a  rapamycin-treated  HEK293  cell  lysate  identified multiple FKBP1A-derived peptides among the most significant hits (FC > 1.5, p-value < 0.01, n = 4 replicates; Figure 2e), as we have previously shown (Piazza et al., 2020).

We observed some variability in the specific  peptide  that  showed  altered protease accessibility  upon  rapamycin  treatment.  One  in-cell  LiP  detected  FKBP1A  peptide (Figure  S4c,  d)  also  showed  changes  in  the  lysate,  whereas  the  other  in-cell  LiP detected  peptide  (Figure  2c,  d)  did  not.  In  both  standard  and  in-cell  LiP,  several proteins  besides  FKBP1  showed  structural  changes  after  rapamycin  treatment (100 nM rapamycin, 5 minutes); we identified 333 (out of 3534 detected) structurally changing proteins with in-cell LiP, and 141 structurally changing proteins (out of 3183 detected) with the standard in-lysate method. Interestingly, the overlap between these sets of proteins was small; only 30 proteins changed structurally in both experiments (Figure S4b) despite good overlap in coverage. This could be because in-lysate LiP may detect target engagement and off-target effects whereas in-cell LiP data may be dominated by effects downstream of the targets. In the in-cell LiP experiment of cells treated with 10 μM rapamycin for 10 minutes, 60 proteins changed structurally (Figure S4b).

The percentage of HT peptides was lower for in-cell LiP than for classical LiP in the native lysate (Figure S4e, f), indicating that there is less PK cleavage in the in-cell context. In this comparison, we used a similar ratio of protein substrate and PK in both conditions: i.e., 100 μg protein and 35 pmol PK (1 μg) in the lysate and 3 million cells (800-1000 μg protein) and 12 nmol (347 μg) PK for in-cell LiP. The  lower  intracellular  cleavage  could  be  the  result  of  lower intracellular  diffusion  due  to  crowding  and  the  presence  of  the  cytoskeleton,  thus reducing access of PK to the proteome. Moreover, there is likely to be a distribution of PK uptake,  with  many  cells  likely  internalizing  only  low  amounts  or  no  PK. Next,  we  asked  whether  we  could  use  our  data  to  detect  proteins  that  undergo structural alterations upon cell lysis, reasoning that this set of proteins would benefit in particular from the in-cell setup. To identify these proteins, we compared the DMSO-

treated control samples of our parallel in-cell LiP and in-lysate LiP experiments.  We found overall a relatively good correlation of peptide intensities (Pearson correlation coefficient ρ = 0.70; Figure 3a), indicating that many proteins retain their structure upon cell lysis at least in these experimental conditions. This was similar for both fully-tryptic (ρ = 0.71) and half-tryptic (ρ = 0.69) peptides (Figure S5a), and for most peptides the ratio of intensities in in-cell LiP to lysate LiP was close to 1 (Figure S5b). Nevertheless, there  were  several  proteins  or  protein  regions  with  relatively  poor  correlation  (i.e., peptides that are not on the diagonal of the correlation plot) and we investigated these proteins further.

To test whether peptides with low correlation between in-cell LiP and classical LiP share common features such as a subcellular localization or function, we calculated a protein-level score of similarity between in-cell LiP and classical LiP by averaging the peptide ratios (for proteins >3 peptides). Interestingly, proteins that differ most between the methods (protein score < 0.9 or > 1.1) are located in mitochondria based on the EBI human GOA database (release 2018-02-20; Figure 3b), whereas proteins in the cytosol, nucleus, vesicles and the Golgi apparatus show similar LiP-based structural fingerprints in both methods (protein score > 0.9 and < 1.1; Figure 3c). Correlation at the peptide level was also lower in mitochondria than in other organelles (ρ = 0.65; Figure  S5c),  and  peptide  ratios  between  in-cell  LiP  and  classical  LiP  revealed  two distinct  peaks  for  mitochondrial  proteins  (Figure  3d).  In  contrast,  peptide  ratios  of proteins located in other organelles exhibit a single peak with an in-cell:lysate intensity ratio close to 1 (Figure S5d). This indicates two populations of mitochondrial proteins that are differentially cleaved by PK.  Since most mitochondrial proteins are encoded in the nucleus, translated in the cytosol, and post-translationally translocated into the mitochondrial  compartments,  where  they  fold,  this  may  reflect  the  cytosolic  and mitochondrial fractions of mitochondrial proteins. In classical  LiP  experiments performed in lysates, both fractions should be equally accessible to PK. In the in-cell context  however,  only  the  cytosolic  and  nuclear  fractions  may  be  accessible,  in particular  since  the  double  mitochondrial  membrane  may  limit  PK  uptake  upon electroporation. Consistent with this, there was no enrichment of the term 'mitochondria' within PK-cleaved proteins in the in-cell LiP setup (Fig 1f).

In-cell LiP-MS identifies structural changes in stress granules

Our data on the rapamycin-FKB1A interaction showed that the in-cell LIP approach can detect structural alterations in proteins within the cell induced by a high-affinity interaction, which is also detectable after cell lysis. The promise of an in-cell method

2

for detecting structural alterations is that it may also be able to capture the dynamics of  biomolecular condensates that are too complex to properly reconstitute in vitro, and that may be partly lost upon cell lysis (Jain et al., 2016).We therefore asked whether in-cell LiP-MS can detect structural changes of proteins that interact in stress granules. Stress  granules  are  cytosolic  biomolecular  condensates  composed  of  proteins  and RNA that can be observed in response to various stresses. Their formation is probably at  least  partly  driven  by  liquid-liquid  phase  separation  via  low  affinity  interactions (Glauninger et  al.,  2022).  We  treated  HEK293  cells  with  sodium  arsenite,  which  is known to induce the formation of stress granules, and incubated them under these stress conditions for up to 90 minutes. At different time points (0, 10, 20 and 90 minutes after arsenite treatment), cells were detached in PBS pre-warmed to 37 °C, centrifuged at 200 x g for 30 seconds, resuspended in PBS, immediately electroporated with PK and then processed by in-cell LiP. We confirmed that stress granules formed between 10 and 20 minutes of arsenite treatment using immunofluorescence staining for the stress granule marker G3BP1 (Figure 4a), which is in line with previous observations (Jain et al., 2016).

After 10 minutes of treatment, before stress granules were observed by microscopy, we detected structural changes in only three peptides from three proteins in arsenitetreated  samples  relative  to  untreated  control;  these  were  Creatine  kinase  B-type (CKB), Protein phosphatase 1 regulatory subunit 12A (PPP1R12A), and SERPINE1 mRNA-binding protein 1 (SERBP1) (Figure 4b; FC>1.5, q-value < 0.05). At 20 minutes of arsenite treatment, 2325 peptides on 1149 proteins had changed significantly, rising to 20,841 peptides on 3146 proteins at 90 minutes of treatment. With few exceptions (1 of 4204 proteins at 10 min, 13 of 4207 proteins at 90 min arsenite treatment), protein levels did not change significantly in this time frame (Figure S6a; FC > 1.5, q-value < 0.05, n = 6 replicates), and we therefore did not globally normalize protein structural changes for protein abundance changes.

Gene ontology enrichment analysis showed that proteins that had changed structure after  20 minutes  and  90 minutes  of  arsenite  treatment  were  indeed  enriched  in components of stress granules (Figure 4c). Moreover, proteins involved in RNA binding and RNA-related biological processes, again associated with stress granules, were also significantly enriched (Figure S6b, c). G3BP1 consists of a NTF2-like domain, which is required for dimerization (Figure 5b), an RNA recognition motif, and three intrinsically disordered regions (IDRs). Both dimerization and RNA binding by G3BP1 are essential for stress granule assembly (Sanders et al., 2020). It has been suggested that G3BP1 adopts a closed conformation in the absence of stress, held together by electrostatic interactions between IDR1 and IDR3. When free RNA levels increase during arsenite stress, the interaction between the IDRs is disrupted and RNA binds to the RNA recognition motif and. The RNA-bound open state of G3BP1 serves as the core of the stress granule and leads to condensation (Yang et al., 2020). After 20 minutes of arsenite treatment, we detected only one altered LiP peptide on G3BP1: this peptide is located exactly on the RNA recognition motif of the protein (Figure 5c), in line with this proposed model of structural rearrangements making the RNA binding domain accessible upon stress. At 90 minutes, structural changes occurred within all domains of G3BP1 (Figure 5c). More broadly, in-cell LiP-MS identified novel structural changes in 190 of 200 high confidence stress granule-associated proteins (based on the RNAgranuleDB v2.0 data base)  that  are  also  MS-detectable  (Millar  et  al.,  2023).  We  obtained  peptide-level resolution  of  protein  structure  dynamics,  as  shown  for  35  stress  granule  proteins selected manually for their special interest (Figure S7). Although stress granules are not detected by microscopy until after 20 minutes of arsenite treatment, significantly changed LiP peptides were detected on three proteins, SERBP1, PPP1R12A, and CKB, after 10 minutes (Figure 5a). PPP1R12A associates with stress granules (Yang et al., 2020). SERBP1 acts as a ribosome dormancy or preservation factor upon mTOR phosphorylation (Shetty et al., 2023) and, upon arsenite treatment, localizes to both stress granules and nucleoli (Lee et al., 2014). SERBP1 has also been implicated in clearance of stress granules after arsenite stress by modulating the degradation of G3BP1  (Wang  et  al.,  2023).  To  our  knowledge,  CKB  has  not  been  previously associated  with  stress  granules.  Thus,  we  provide  information  on  the  structure dynamics of novel and known stress granule proteins during arsenite stress, with many changes mapping to known disordered and RNA binding regions (Figure 6). Interestingly, gene ontology analysis also suggested that nuclear speckles, another membraneless biomolecular condensate, and the spliceosome, which associates with nuclear  speckles,  are  enriched  among  proteins  that  show  structural  changes  after 20 minutes  of  arsenite  treatment.  At  this  time  point,  we  observed  changes  in  80 proteins  that  are  associated  with  nuclear  speckles  (based  on  gene  ontology annotations), including 14 splicing factors, and the phosphatase PP1-gamma catalytic subunit, which has been reported to dynamically re-localize between cytoplasm and nuclear speckles (Trinkle-Mulcahy et al., 2001). After 90 min, 161 proteins located in nuclear speckles showed evidence of structural changes, including the known marker protein SC35. To assess whether these in-cell LiP detected structural alterations of nuclear speckle proteins detected by in-cell LiP reflected morphological changes in this phase-separated compartment, we monitored arsenite-treated cells with fluorescence microscopy after staining for the known nuclear speckle marker SC35 (Figure 7a). We assessed the circularity of nuclear speckles, which is known to change upon alterations in the dynamics of this compartment. We found that nuclear speckles became rounder upon sodium arsenite treatment in comparison to untreated control cells (Figure 7a, b). This effect is in line with reported changes of nuclear speckle morphology upon other stresses (J. Kim et al., 2019; Raina & Rao, 2022; Spector et al., 1991). Taken together, our data show that in-cell LiP identified both known and novel alterations in proteins connected to biomolecular condensates within human cells. Further, we have used this approach to generate a compendium of proteins undergoing structural alterations upon arsenite stress, and to identify structurally altered regions in these proteins.

Discussion

We present in-cell LiP-MS, a novel method to study proteome-scale structural changes in their native environment. Compared to LiP-MS in lysates, intracellular organization and molecular concentrations are preserved in this approach, enabling the study of changes in cellular physiology with minimal invasion. Since LiP-MS captures functional molecular  events  such  as  enzyme  activity,  protein-protein  interaction,  PTM  and allosteric regulation, as we have previously shown (Cappelletti et al., 2021), the in-cell version of this technique should prove a powerful method for various types of functional proteomics studies.

Although some existing structural proteomic methods can be implemented in intact cells,  they  face  challenges.  Hydroxyl  radical  footprinting  (HRF)-MS  reports  on intracellular structural changes with residue-level resolution, but requires a microfluidic setup and oxidations are only seen in approximately 100 proteins (Espino et al., 2015).

Crosslinking mass spectrometry applied to intact cells also suffers from low proteome coverage.  Thermal  protein  profiling  (TPP)  uses  a  simple  temperature  gradient  to identify proteins that change stability under conditions of interest and does not suffer from low coverage, but it  lacks structural  information  since  it does  not  pinpoint  the region(s) of a protein that may be altered (Savitski et al., 2014). Further, our approach has advantages over in-cell NMR, since in-cell LiP is applied on proteome-scale and does not require labeling. However, the two approaches may be complementary since in-cell LiP does not achieve the atomic resolution of NMR. Structurally altered proteins that are detected by in-cell LiP in an unbiased manner can be followed up by in-cell NMR.

We expect that modification of several parameters could help extend our approach in the  future.  With  the  current  settings,  we  identified  several  thousands  of  proteins, however,  sequence  coverage  per  protein  remained  medium  to  low  in  most  cases. While  coverage  was  not  increased  with  higher  PK  concentration  and  prolonged incubation  time,  in  applications  where  cells  do  not  need  to  remain  viable  after electroporation, higher electroporation voltage and pulse number may lead to more uniform influx of PK and possibly to better sequence coverage.

In-cell LiP-MS may be adapted to other temperatures, pH, or different low-specificity proteases. It  may  even  be  feasible  to introduce an  inactivated protease  by electroporation, such that the start of proteolysis can be more tightly controlled. For example,  the  catalytic  residues  of  a  protease  may  be  replaced  with  a  photocaged homologue such that it can be light-activated under conditions of interest. Further, incell LiP could be used not only to cell lines, and may be applicable also to tissues and to unicellular organisms such as bacteria or yeast. The FRET-based proteolysis assay we have described here should prove useful to screen for optimal conditions to adapt in-cell  LiP  to  other  systems.  The  reporter  may  also  be  used  to  test  whether experimental conditions affect PK activity which, as in classical LiP-MS, can affect the interpretation of an experiment.

We have optimized in-cell LiP-MS conditions to preserve the native state of cells as far as possible. For example, care should be taken that cells do not clump after incubation with  extracellular  PK,  most  probably  due  to  partial  proteolysis  of  surface  proteins. Clumping  during  limited  proteolysis  can  be  avoided  if  the  added  PK  is  ice-cold, electroporation is performed within seconds after mixing PK and cells, and cells are resuspended well prior to addition of PK. Good cell resuspension also results in higher PK uptake. Mixing after protease addition should be performed within seconds and further mixing should be avoided.

In classical LiP-MS on lysates, optimal coverage has been found for 1 μg PK added to 100 μg  substrate  protein.  For  in-cell  LiP,  we  obtained  the  highest  coverage  by electroporating 3 million HEK293 cells with 12 nmol PK, of which 36.6 pmol PK were intracellular. If different cell types or electroporation settings are used, we recommend first screening for conditions that yield good PK internalization using the FRET reporter, and then optimizing the conditions using the % half-tryptic peptides or the number of PK cleavages in the proteome as a readout. Coverage can then be determined using mass spectrometry by comparing cells electroporated with PK to control cells to which PK was added but not electroporated. Another difference between in-cell and in-lysate LiP is the homogeneity of the system: in lysates, an almost homogeneous mixture of proteins, PK and any other added perturbant can be achieved, whereas in-cell LiP analyzes a mixture of cells which may be in different cellular states, and PK uptake may vary. Perhaps as a result, reproducibility is currently higher in classical LiP, which in  part  can be compensated by using more replicates in in-cell LiP. Because exact timing matters in LiP, up to eight lysates were treated and processed simultaneously. In  our  current  setup,  the  electroporation  equipment  limits us  to  electroporating one sample at a time and thus we anticipate that increasing the throughput of this step would improve reproducibility.

Classical LiP-MS can be used to identify drug targets in cell lysates. The in-cell LiP approach  detected  the  binding  of  rapamycin  to  FKBP1A,  its  only  known  target  in mammalian  cells,  showing  that  the  method  detects  a  conformational  change  on  a single target protein in a very complex and crowded background. While the effect was smaller than seen for LiP-MS in lysates, this could be because of sample heterogeneity or  because  protease  accessibility  of  FKBP1A  may  be  different  in  the  crowded environment of the cell.   Moreover, the intracellular target might be less saturated than in the lysate where the drug is added in excess to a homogeneous protein suspension.

In-cell LiP-MS should have the ability to identify not only direct binding partners but also  downstream  effects,  which  may  be  enhanced  and  separated  from  targetengagement effects with time-course experiments.  This opens the exciting possibility that  the  approach  could  be  used  to  identify  drug  targets  or  targeted  pathways, downstream events, and off-target effects within intact cells under specific conditions of interest, e.g., hypoxia. We have previously shown that dose-response experiments can help rank potential binding partners in classical LiP-MS, and we envision that such a strategy could be applied in the in-cell context as well. Dose-  and time-resolved drug profiling of post-translational modifications (PTMs) has been used to group proteins involved in different downstream events (Zecha et al., 2023). This approach allowed to elucidate the mode of action of drugs in cells and even to assign PTMs to pathways.

For drugs that trigger fast intracellular responses, in-cell LiP analyses may result in large sets of hits even at early time points, which could preclude the separation of direct binding and downstream events. In such cases, target engagement events can be prioritized by LiP-MS experiments performed in parallel on drug-treated cell lysates. In-cell LiP-MS could also help to elucidate effects of mutations in pathways affected by drug  binding,  as  well  as  the  effect  of  two  or  more  drugs  added  simultaneously  or subsequently to cells.

The study also generated a resource that pinpoints proteins whose structure is affected if they are studied outside the cellular environment, and on RNA and DNA interacting domains that are altered upon cell lysis. Developing organelle-specific in cell LiP by targeting the protease to the different intracellular locations could be an interesting direction to detect organelle-specific structural states and in general to probe protein structures in subcellular compartments that are currently not accessible to our setup.

Application of in-cell LiP-MS to arsenite-stressed cells provides to our knowledge the first in  situ insight  into  structural  rearrangements  of  stress  granule  proteins.  These dynamic structures have typically been studied by fluorescence microscopy, which lacks structural resolution, or by in  vitro reconstitution,  but  until  now  there  were  no methods available to study structure information in large dynamic assemblies. Stress granule proteins, including the scaffolding protein G3BP1, were enriched in the set of structurally altered proteins upon arsenite stress, suggesting that the dataset may be used to generate hypotheses about so far unknown components or regulators of stress granules.  Information  on  the  specific  protein  regions  involved  may  shed  light  on mechanisms of stress granule formation. Overall, these data provide the first resource on proteome-scale and time-resolved structural changes in human cells under stress with peptide-level resolution.

Of  special  interest  are  the  structural  changes  in  CKB,  PPP1R12A  and  SERBP1, detected before stress granules are visible by fluorescence microscopy. The enzyme Creatine  kinase  B-type  (CKB)  plays  an  important  role  in  energy  transfer  and homeostasis  and  has  not  so  far  been  linked  to  stress  granules.  CKB  transfers phosphate from ATP to creatine. The resulting phosphocreatine serves as donor of the phosphate group to rapidly regenerate ATP from ADP. Although the observed changes could be in principle caused by sodium arsenite unrelated to stress granules, the timing suggests that the protein might be involved in the stress granule formation. The Protein phosphatase  1  regulatory  subunit  12A  (PPP1R12A)  is  involved  in  various  cellular processes including muscle contraction and cell cycle regulation, where it antagonizes Polo-like kinase 1 (PLK1), an essential regulator of mitosis (Yamashiro et al., 2008). PPP1R12A has been detected as part of stress granules (Yang et al., 2020) but its functional role is unknown; our data suggest that it may play a role in the early stages of stress granule formation and pinpoints the regions involved. The SERPINE1 mRNAbinding protein 1 (SERBP1), is known to interact with stress-granule proteins upon arsenite  treatment  in  an  RNA-dependent  manner  (Lee  et  al.,  2014)  and  has  been described to be essential for stress granule clearance after arsenite stress (Wang et al., 2023). The same study found that knocking out SERBP1 had no effect on stress granules after 60 min of arsenite treatment, consistent with our observation of an earlier structural change in this protein. It is also possible that the structural change we detect in SERBP1 occurs in the nuclear-located protein, which is known to increase in level upon arsenite stress, and where it likely it binds to mRNA free ribosomes to stabilize the 80S particle (Shetty et al., 2023), or due to the relocation itself (Lee et al., 2014).

Nuclear  speckles  are  dynamic  assemblies  of  splicing  factors  that  were  not  yet implicated  in  the  response  to  arsenite  stress,  but  we  observed  enrichment  of  both speckle proteins and the splicing machinery among structurally altered proteins upon arsenite treatment. In line with this, we could observe morphological changes in these compartments with  fluorescence microscopy, which have previously been seen upon heat shock (Spector et al., 1991), upon inhibition of transcription (T. Kim et al., 2023), and upon inhibition of the DEAD-box ATPase UAP56 (Hondele et al., 2019). Further analysis of the set of structurally altered proteins may provide insight into mechanisms leading  to  the  altered  morphology  of  nuclear  speckles  and  the  molecular  players involved.

In  the  future  we  envision  a  broad  range of applications of in-cell LiP  in  the field of condensates. For example, our data may be used to identify LiP marker peptides for stress granule formation, potentially including markers that probe the involvement of the RNA binding and IDP regions for each protein. Similar markers could be identified for the formation of other physiological molecular condensates (such as Cajal bodies or p-bodies) or metabolons  (e.g. the purinosome)  and  even  for  pathological condensates  (e.g.  from  amyloidogenic  proteins  such  as  FUS  or  TDP43).  These markers, potentially measured simultaneously with a targeted MS analysis, could be used  to  study  the  detailed  dynamics  of  such  structures  within  the  cell,  identify perturbations that influence them, and study potential cross-talk between condensates. One may envision using such markers to derive in cell phase diagrams of condensates.

Other applications of the technique beyond condensates may include the characterization of the global cellular response to changes in pH, such as occurs in the tumor microenvironment, or to other environmental factors such as toxins or irradiation. In-cell LiP may provide mechanistic information into viral entry, receptor internalization upon drug binding, the binding of modified T-cells in immunotherapy, or nutrient shifts. Another exciting application of the approach is the study of altered material properties of the cytosol,  e.g. the phenomenon of extreme cytosol rigidification upon starvation and  quiescence  (Joyner  et  al.,  2016;  Munder  et  al.,  2016).  The  nature  of  protein structures in this state is still unknown; they may phase separate or aggregate, this may involve all proteins or only a subset, and RNA-binding, intrinsically disordered or other protein domains could be involved. In-cell LiP-MS detected structural changes could shed light on all of these questions.

In summary, we have shown that in-cell LiP-MS can detect proteome-wide structural changes upon a perturbation of interest, within the native environment of the intact cell and at peptide resolution. Since protein structural changes are likely to be ubiquitous under perturbation or stimulus of a biological system, in-cell LiP-MS has the potential to provide insight into numerous cellular processes including labile assemblies formed by phase separation that could so far not be addressed with established methods.

metadata.preprint_title: Limited proteolysis-coupled mass spectrometry captures proteome-wide protein structural alterations and biomolecular condensation in living cells

pub_metadata.preprint_title: Limited proteolysis-coupled mass spectrometry captures proteome-wide protein structural alterations and biomolecular condensation in living cells
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: Elsässer, F.; Florea, R.; Räsch, F.; Weis, K.; de Souza, N.; Picotti, P.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract: The function of a protein is determined by its structure, which may change dynamically in response to post-translational modifications, interaction with other molecules, or environmental factors like temperature. Limited proteolysis-coupled mass spectrometry (LiP-MS) captures such structural alterations on a proteome-wide scale via the detection of altered protease susceptibility patterns of proteins. However, this technique has so far required cell lysis, which exposes proteins to non-native conditions and can disrupt labile interactions such as those occurring within biomolecular condensates. To study protein structures directly within cells, we developed in-cell LiP-MS. We optimized conditions for introduction of proteinase K into human cells using electroporation and validated that intracellular cleavage occurs. In-cell LiP-MS captured not only the known binding of rapamycin to FKBP1A within the cell, but also downstream effects of pathway activation. Moreover, it detected global protein structural alterations upon sodium arsenite treatment and captured the structural dynamics of hundreds of proteins from biomolecular condensates with peptide level resolution and within live human cells. We detected known and novel structural alterations of proteins from stress granules as well as from nuclear speckles and validated alteration of nuclear speckles by fluorescence microscopy. Our dataset provides a resource describing the structural changes of human proteins in response to cellular stress and pinpoints structurally altered regions. Further, comparison of LiP-based structural fingerprints before and after cell lysis revealed which human proteins are susceptible to structural change upon cell lysis, therefore guiding the design of future experiments requiring native protein structures.
